title: RFC 3161 TSA call blocks audit-log latency SLA (NFR-LAT-009)
feature: Panel-action audit log write with cryptographic timestamp
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-009 budgets 500 ms p95 for panel-action log write (review comment, flag, decision state change). This is a critical-tier SLA for forensic audit trail. RFC 3161 Time Stamp Authority round-trip adds 80-150 ms to each write. If TSA call is synchronous, p95 latency will exceed 500 ms budget (500 ms - 100 ms reserved for network/app = 400 ms available, but 80-150 ms TSA + 100-150 ms immutable-log-write = 180-300 ms taken, leaving only 100-200 ms for actual app work and validation). If TSA call is asynchronous (fire-and-forget), audit trail is weakened: if process crashes before TSA response arrives, cryptographic timestamp is lost. Incident investigation depends on provable evidence of decision timing; a weak audit trail defeats forensic accountability.
recommendation: Use asynchronous TSA call with durable queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ) to decouple user-facing latency from TSA round-trip. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff and dead-letter handling. Monitor TSA availability and set SLA alert if TSA response time exceeds 100 ms or availability drops below 99.5%. Use local clock to timestamp at time of action, then backfill cryptographic TSA token asynchronously. Test impact with synthetic load: validate that p95 NFR-LAT-009 remains <500 ms with async TSA. Conduct incident drills to verify forensic audit trail completeness after process crash scenarios.

title: OAuth 2.1 IdP outage becomes single point of failure (F-01 to F-08 workflows blocked)
feature: OAuth 2.1 / OIDC authentication with MOD + CAA IdP
reasoning: CAA Workbench spans civil (CAA IdP) and military (MOD IdP) regulatory boundaries. Every user session begins with IdP authentication. If MOD IdP is unavailable (network partition, IdP server failure), all classified-tier users (panel members, SORA assessors) cannot authenticate, and Noyce F-05 through F-08 workflows (case-open, AI-flag narrative, evidence navigation, audit-log write) are blocked. If CAA IdP fails, civil applicants cannot upload evidence, blocking F-01 through F-03 workflows. IdP latency degrades NFR-LAT-001 (applicant workspace creation): if IdP p95 latency is 150 ms instead of budgeted 100 ms, workspace creation p95 exceeds 3-second SLA. Parent story DoD specifies 'live system red-team exercises'; if red-team successfully performs IdP DoS attack (or targets IdP infrastructure), workbench becomes operationally unavailable during critical live panel window. This is an unmitigated availability crisis.
recommendation: Implement IdP redundancy: primary + secondary + tertiary IdP endpoints across geographic regions (MOD: 3-region failover; CAA: 3-region failover). Cache OAuth tokens for 1-hour TTL with refresh-token rotation. Implement graceful degradation: if primary IdP is unavailable for >30 seconds, serve read-only cached token to low-risk users (applicants with view-only access). For critical panel workflows (F-05 to F-08), require fresh token but implement 5-second IdP timeout + fallback to 'limited panel mode' (no new evidence imports, no new AI-flag narratives, read-only case review). Test IdP failover scenarios quarterly. Conduct synthetic monitoring of both IdP endpoints with separate SLA thresholds. Coordinate with MOD/CAA to ensure IdP infrastructure is hardened against nation-state DDoS (GCAP/Tempest are targets). For live red-team exercises, pre-coordinate with IdP teams to prevent unexpected failover during tests.

title: Hash-chain verification overhead risks case-open SLA (NFR-LAT-006 p95 10s)
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-006 'Panel case-open and load' budgets 10 seconds (p95) for regulator to click a case and see interactive panel. Hash-chain verification (ensuring evidence has not been tampered with) is a critical forensic control. Typical 100 MB evidence pack: 50-500 ms to compute SHA-256 hashes (CPU-bound on single thread). If system processes cases sequentially or if CPU contention occurs during peak panel review (10+ simultaneous reviewers per Noyce NFR-LAT-019), hash verification latency could balloon to 1-2 seconds per case. Noyce NFR-LAT-006 component budget for 'evidence_metadata_load' is only 1500 ms; hash verification cannot consume >200 ms without risking p95 overrun. Incident investigation (AAIB, Service Inquiry) depends on provable evidence integrity; cannot skip hash verification, but parallel computation is essential.
recommendation: Implement parallel SHA-256 verification using worker threads (1 thread per 10 MB chunk, or use GPU acceleration if available). Pre-compute hashes during evidence upload (Noyce NFR-LAT-002) and cache in fast-access tier (Redis, in-process cache). Use cryptographic hash acceleration (AES-NI, SHA-NI CPU extensions) if available on compute platform. Target hash verification latency: <200 ms p95 for 100 MB typical case, <500 ms p95 for 500 MB worst-case pack (Noyce NFR-LAT-018). Implement incremental verification: verify hashes in background while rendering case panel (non-blocking, alert user if verification fails). Conduct stress-testing with 10+ simultaneous case-opens to measure hash verification contention. If p95 exceeds 200 ms under load, scale hash-verification pool horizontally.

title: Classification marking validation overhead scales poorly with search results (NFR-LAT-017)
feature: Classification boundary check during evidence search across 10-year archive
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-017 'Evidence search across classification boundary' budgets 5 seconds (p95) for a query returning multiple results across 10-year archive. Classification boundary check (ensuring user's clearance level permits access to each result) is required per result. If query returns 1000 results and classification check is 5 ms per result, total overhead is 5 seconds alone, consuming the entire SLA budget before search index lookup or result rendering. Workbench stores 10 years of trial records (estimated 10,000+ evidence items per year = 100,000+ items searchable). Classification boundary logic is non-negotiable (UK OFFICIAL/OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE/SECRET segregation per parent NFR), but lazy evaluation (checking classification after result fetching) could degrade UX if checks block result rendering.
recommendation: Pre-filter evidence by user's classification clearance level at search index level (Elasticsearch, Solr) rather than post-query filtering. Index evidence with clearance-level tags (OFFICIAL, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET) and use index-level access control to eliminate ineligible results before retrieval. Cache classification-clearance decisions per user session (TTL = 1 hour) to amortize repeated lookups. For bulk export (decision record PDF, Noyce NFR-LAT-010), batch classification checks asynchronously. Test search latency with realistic 10-year archive size (100,000+ items); measure p95 latency scaling with result count. Set performance regression alert if classification boundary check pushes p95 search latency >5 seconds.

title: DPIA redaction blocks decision-record export SLA (REQ012, Noyce NFR-LAT-010)
feature: MOR/DPIA-driven PII redaction on decision-record PDF export
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-010 'Decision record export as PDF' budgets 300 seconds (p95) for a complete PDF with evidence citations, panel decisions, and audit summary. DPIA-required redaction (removing witness names, applicant PII, addresses per Data Protection Act 2018 / UK GDPR) adds significant CPU overhead. PII pattern matching (regex or NLP-based) on large text corpus (100+ MB evidence) can consume 50-200 ms per pattern check. If DPIA redaction rules are complex (20+ patterns for different PII types), total redaction latency could be 1-4 seconds per export. Parent NFR specifies handling OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE material (which contains names of applicants and witnesses); redaction is non-negotiable but latency-sensitive. Simple redaction (list of known witness names) is fast; complex redaction (identifying unknown PII via ML) is slow.
recommendation: Implement two-tier redaction: fast-path (whitelist of known PII fields: witness names, contact info from submission metadata) and slow-path (ML-based PII detection for unknown entities). For fast-path redaction, pre-extract PII at evidence upload time and cache redaction list. Offer fast-export option (simple redaction, <60 seconds SLA) for common cases and slow-export option (comprehensive redaction, <5 minutes SLA) for complex cases. Implement async redaction: generate unredacted PDF immediately, redact in background, notify user when complete. For 500 MB worst-case evidence pack (Noyce NFR-LAT-018), estimate redaction latency and implement streaming redaction to avoid memory exhaustion. Test DPIA redaction latency with realistic witness/applicant data (100-500 entities per case); measure p95 latency and ensure it does not dominate NFR-LAT-010 budget.

title: Applicant portal DDoS attack surface: credential-stuffing + large-payload uploads
feature: Applicant portal: login endpoint + evidence upload endpoint
reasoning: Applicant portal (OFFICIAL-tier auth gate, Noyce F-01 to F-03) is the only public-facing workbench entry point. Two high-value DoS targets: (1) Login endpoint: credential-stuffing attack using leaked password databases (e.g., LinkedIn breach). Rate limiting is 5/min per account, 20/min per IP. An attacker with 1000 botnet IPs can sustain 20,000 login attempts/min, overwhelming rate-limiting thresholds. (2) Evidence upload endpoint: attacker uploads large malicious PDFs (500 MB each) to exhaust storage quota and detector compute. Noyce NFR-LAT-002 budgets detector to complete in 10 seconds; crafted PDF with embedded ZIP bomb or malicious VBA could trigger exponential decompression, hanging detector and blocking legitimate uploads. Parent story DoD mentions 'nation-state adversaries' and GCAP/Tempest targets; workbench is in-scope for adversary interest.
recommendation: Implement multi-layer DDoS defense: (1) CDN-level DDoS mitigation (100+ Gbps capacity, UK-sovereign provider). (2) WAF rules to detect and block credential-stuffing patterns (sudden spike in login failures from diverse IPs). (3) CAPTCHA challenge after 3 failed login attempts per account. (4) Exponential backoff: lock account for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts. (5) Implement timeout on PDF scanning (Noyce 'virus_scan' and 'content_classification' components): if detector does not respond in 5 seconds, reject upload and queue for offline analysis. (6) Per-applicant upload quota: 2 concurrent uploads, max 500 MB each, max 2 GB total per case. (7) Monitor upload rate by applicant ID and source IP; alert if >1 upload/second from single applicant or >100 uploads/minute from single IP. Conduct DDoS simulation with red-team: attempt credential-stuffing and upload-bombing during live panel window; measure impact on workbench latency and applicant availability. Coordinate with CDN provider to establish emergency escalation path if DDoS breaches mitigation capacity.

title: Classification boundary service vulnerable to slow-loris attack during live panel
feature: Classification boundary check in evidence search (Noyce NFR-LAT-017)
reasoning: Classification boundary checker is a dedicated microservice (per Noyce architecture) called for every evidence search and panel-action authorization. Slow-loris attack (attacker sends incomplete HTTP requests, holding connections open) could exhaust server connection pool, blocking legitimate searches and panel decisions. Parent story DoD specifies red-team exercises against live system during panel operation. If red-team performs coordinated slow-loris attack at panel-facing boundary-check endpoint (which would be known to red-team during architecture review), panel members could lose ability to navigate evidence or log actions, effectively halting case review. Noyce NFR-LAT-006 'case-open' and NFR-LAT-008 'evidence navigation' both depend on responsive boundary-check service.
recommendation: Implement request timeout (30-second read timeout) at HTTP layer to drop incomplete requests. Use connection pooling with configurable connection limits (e.g., max 1000 concurrent connections). Implement connection-limit per source IP (e.g., max 10 concurrent connections per IP). Deploy reverse-proxy (nginx, HAProxy) in front of boundary-check service with slow-loris mitigation enabled. Monitor connection state histogram; alert if >5% of connections are in incomplete-request state. Implement adaptive timeouts: if connection queue is growing, reduce timeout from 30s to 10s to force faster cleanup. Conduct red-team exercise targeting slow-loris attack; measure impact on NFR-LAT-006 and NFR-LAT-008 latency. If p95 latency exceeds SLA during slow-loris attack, implement circuit-breaker: if boundary-check response time exceeds 2 seconds, serve cached classification decision (with staleness warning).

title: Clause library search endpoint susceptible to enumeration attack
feature: Clause library read-only endpoint (OFFICIAL-tier auth gate)
reasoning: Clause library is public read-only content (regulatory clauses, guidance documents) available to all authenticated users. Rate limiting is 100 requests/min per user. An attacker with compromised or brute-forced credentials could enumerate entire clause library by performing sequential queries (e.g., search for each term from dictionary). Over 10 minutes, attacker can issue 1000 queries, potentially downloading entire library. Clause library is not classified SECRET, but CAA may want to limit unauthorized mass-download. Rate limiting is defensive but not airtight (100/min is high; attacker can download ~100 KB clauses/min, feasible for small database).
recommendation: Rate limiting of 100 req/min per user is reasonable baseline. Implement additional safeguards: (1) Bulk-export endpoint: offer authorized users a full-dump download link (updated monthly) rather than forcing enumeration queries. (2) Implement exponential backoff: after user hits rate limit, increase cooldown from 60s to 5 minutes. (3) Implement geolocation-based anomaly detection: flag searches from unusual geographic locations (e.g., attacker in hostile nation). (4) Log clause-search patterns; alert if single user downloads >50% of clause library in <1 hour. (5) Implement query complexity limits: block queries with >10 disjunctive terms (potential relevance-ranking DoS). No urgent action needed; low severity due to public nature of clause content.

title: Frozen-model manifest validation latency could degrade autonomy inference (NFR-LAT-015)
feature: Onboard ML model validation (SHA-256 frozen-model hash)
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-015 'Onboard ML inference for autonomy decisions' budgets 200 ms (p95) for tactical ML decisions (waypoint selection, DAA evasion). Frozen-model hash verification (REQ009 forensic explainability requirement) adds ~50 ms on ARM Cortex compute. If model validation is synchronous and called before every inference, SLA is tight: 200 ms - 50 ms (hash) = 150 ms for actual inference, which is constrained but achievable with quantized models. However, if model validation fails (hash mismatch due to firmware corruption), system must trigger failsafe (flight termination). Failsafe latency is safety-critical (Noyce NFR-LAT-016 'Flight termination' budgets 500 ms), so hash validation must be <100 ms to leave room for failsafe execution. On slower compute platforms (ARM Cortex-A9), SHA-256 validation could exceed 100 ms, delaying failsafe response.
recommendation: Implement model validation at mission-startup time, not at each inference call. Pre-load frozen-model hash into secure element (HSM or eFuse) during mission upload. At each inference call, do lightweight hash check (compare pre-computed hash against cached value in secure memory). This reduces per-inference overhead from 50 ms to <1 ms. For hash validation at startup, allocate 5 seconds (long-running operation, not time-critical). Test model validation latency on target compute hardware (ARM Cortex-A72, Cortex-A53); measure hash verification time and ensure startup validation completes within 5 seconds. Log any hash mismatch as security event and trigger immediate flight-termination failsafe.

title: WAF ruleset depth trades latency vs. threat coverage (applicant portal)
feature: WAF inspection on applicant portal ingress
reasoning: WAF overhead estimated at 10-30 ms depending on ruleset depth. OWASP CRS 3.3.2 baseline ruleset (100+ rules) adds ~10 ms. Customized ruleset for malicious PDF detection (Noyce 'virus_scan' and 'content_classification' are downstream, but WAF can pre-filter obvious payloads) adds another 5-10 ms. Aggressive ruleset (200+ rules) could add 20-30 ms. Noyce NFR-LAT-001 'Applicant workspace creation' budgets 200 ms for 'web_server_auth' component; WAF is upstream, so 20 ms overhead is 10% of that budget. Acceptable but not negligible. Heavy WAF inspection could also cause false positives: legitimate applicant requests (e.g., large JSON evidence metadata) could be blocked as suspicious. False-positive rate must remain <1%.
recommendation: Use OWASP CRS 3.3.2 baseline with paranoia level 2 (balanced coverage + low false-positive rate). Add custom rules for malicious PDF signatures (e.g., suspicious JavaScript, embedded executables) but keep rule count <150. Implement WAF exclusion list for known-legitimate request patterns (e.g., applicant uploads with large Content-Length). Monitor WAF logs for false-positive rate; target <0.5%. Conduct quarterly review of WAF rule effectiveness; retire rules with zero matches. Test WAF latency impact with synthetic load; ensure p95 latency remains <15 ms.
